parsed.title: Effective Reasoning Chains Reduce Intrinsic Dimensionality
parsed.venue_hint: ICML 2026 (Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning)
parsed.field: Natural Language Processing / LLM Reasoning / Analysis of Chain-of-Thought
parsed.abstract: The paper proposes intrinsic dimensionality as a quantitative measure for characterizing the effectiveness of different chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning strategies. By fixing the model architecture and varying the reasoning strategy used to generate training rationales, the authors show that effective reasoning strategies consistently reduce the intrinsic dimensionality (minimum number of trainable parameters, via LoRA, needed to reach a fixed performance threshold) of the task. On GSM8K with Gemma-3 1B and 4B, they find a strong inverse correlation between intrinsic dimensionality and generalization performance on in-distribution and out-of-distribution data, outperforming alternative metrics like length, token perplexity, and KL divergence.

sections.introduction: Motivates the question of when and why CoT reasoning is effective and which reasoning strategy is most effective. Existing explanations (structure, length, process reward models) are either qualitative or yield conflicting evidence. The authors propose intrinsic dimensionality as a principled, information-theoretic measure grounded in minimum description length, showing it strongly inversely correlates with generalization across many CoT variants on GSM8K with Gemma-3 1B/4B.
sections.related_work: Discusses prior analyses of CoT efficacy (Sprague et al., Wang et al., Li et al.) that are mostly non-quantifiable, and proxies for reasoning chain quality (self-consistency, review tokens, forking tokens, ROSCOE, ReCEval). Reviews intrinsic dimension literature (Li et al. 2018, Aghajanyan et al. 2021) and connections to LoRA and MDL. Distinguishes from data-manifold intrinsic dimension estimators.
sections.method: Defines intrinsic dimension d_int = min{d : A(d) >= tau}, where A(d) is the training accuracy of a model trained with d trainable parameters via LoRA, and tau is a common threshold across reasoning strategies. They sweep LoRA rank r and target modules (attention q/v, all attention, all MLP, all layers) to produce ~20-30 configurations uniformly spaced in log-parameter-count. Threshold tau is set as 90% of the maximum epoch-1 full-capacity training accuracy across strategies (with robustness to other thresholds tested).
sections.theory: Argues conceptually from Minimum Description Length: if a reasoning strategy effectively bridges input-to-answer logical gaps, the input-output mapping becomes more compressible, requiring fewer degrees of freedom (lower intrinsic dimension). LoRA serves as an upper-bound proxy for intrinsic dimensionality.
sections.experiments: Datasets: GSM8K training set used to generate 14 different reasoning strategies (No CoT, No CoT+extra tokens, Very Short CoT, Short CoT, Short CoT with 2/4/8 distractors, Gemma 27B CoT, Gemini CoT, Executed PoT, Simulated PoT, Plan and Solve, Critical CoT, High Review Ratio CoT). Evaluations on GSM8K test (ID) plus 5 OOD stress tests (GSM-Symbolic main/P1/P2, GSM-IC, GSM-Hard). Models: Gemma-3 1B and 4B, trained with LoRA over multiple parameter budgets. Compared metrics: intrinsic dimension, trajectory length, token perplexity, sequence KL divergence, LongPPL. Additional experiments on Reasoning Gym (algorithmic + cognitive tasks) and an experiment with 50% incorrect training reasoning chains.
sections.results: Intrinsic dimension Spearman rank correlation with overall accuracy: 0.93 (4B) and 0.75 (1B), both significant. Token PPL: 0.82 (4B), 0.63 (1B). KL divergence: -0.17 / -0.18 (essentially no predictive power). Length: 0.31 / 0.24 (weak). Robustness across thresholds (70%/80%/90% of epoch-1 train acc, 90% val acc) maintains correlations 0.72-0.94. Executed PoT achieves lowest intrinsic dim and best OOD performance. Larger models compress effective strategies more efficiently but show higher intrinsic dim for ineffective ones. Mixing 50% incorrect chains drastically increases intrinsic dim and reduces accuracy. Reasoning Gym shows intrinsic dim is best predictor across algorithmic and cognitive tasks (and notably, No CoT wins for cognitive tasks).
sections.limitations: Estimating intrinsic dimensionality requires many LoRA fine-tunes, making it computationally expensive and not directly optimizable. LoRA provides only an upper bound on true intrinsic dimensionality; the bound may be loose. The measure may reflect artifacts of specific model families or datasets.
sections.conclusion: Effective reasoning chains reduce intrinsic dimensionality, providing a principled, MDL-grounded quantitative predictor of generalization that outperforms perplexity, length, and KL alternatives. Future work includes more tractable proxies, alternative PEFT methods, and applying the framework to other post-training settings such as RL.

main_claims: Intrinsic dimensionality (minimum LoRA-trainable parameters needed to reach a fixed accuracy threshold) is a quantitative measure of reasoning strategy effectiveness.
Effective reasoning strategies reduce the intrinsic dimensionality of the learning task, despite producing longer outputs.
Intrinsic dimensionality strongly inversely correlates with generalization performance (ID and OOD) across CoT variants on GSM8K.
Intrinsic dimensionality is a substantially better predictor of generalization than length, token perplexity, KL divergence, or LongPPL.
Findings are robust to threshold choice and generalize across model sizes (Gemma-3 1B and 4B) and reasoning domains (math and Reasoning Gym tasks).
Executed Program-of-Thought (PoT) attains both the lowest intrinsic dimensionality and the best OOD generalization.
key_contributions: Introducing intrinsic dimensionality (via LoRA sweeps) as a quantitative metric for evaluating CoT reasoning strategies.
An MDL-based theoretical framing connecting compressibility of input-output mappings to generalization in reasoning.
Empirical demonstration on 14 reasoning strategies, two Gemma-3 model sizes, and multiple OOD evaluation sets that intrinsic dimensionality strongly predicts generalization.
Comparative meta-evaluation against length, token perplexity, KL divergence, and LongPPL baselines.
Robustness analyses across threshold selection methods, training data correctness, and reasoning domains (Reasoning Gym).
experimental_results: Gemma-3 4B: intrinsic dim Spearman correlation 0.93 with overall accuracy (vs 0.82 token PPL, 0.31 length, -0.17 KL).
Gemma-3 1B: intrinsic dim Spearman correlation 0.75 (vs 0.63 token PPL, 0.24 length, -0.18 KL).
Executed PoT: lowest intrinsic dim (1.49M for 4B; 1.03M for 1B) and best OOD accuracy (43.40% on 4B).
Threshold robustness: correlations remain 0.72-0.94 across 70%/80%/90% epoch-1 train and 90% val thresholds.
Mixing 50% incorrect Short CoT chains increases intrinsic dim ~500x and drops accuracy by 12 points; for Gemma 27B CoT, ~125x increase and 12.8-point drop.
On Reasoning Gym algorithmic tasks: intrinsic dim correlation = 1.00 with overall accuracy; Executed PoT best (73.21 overall).
On Reasoning Gym cognitive tasks: No CoT achieves lowest intrinsic dim (0.40M) and best performance (49.82); intrinsic dim correlation = 1.00, while length and token PPL show -1.00.
LongPPL variants (K=64, K=256) correlate only 0.60-0.62 with generalization on 4B.
assumptions_and_limitations: Uses LoRA as a proxy for intrinsic dimensionality; this provides only an upper bound and may be loose for some tasks.
Intrinsic dimension is computationally expensive to estimate, requiring many fine-tuning runs.
Training datasets differ slightly in size across reasoning strategies (6.2K-7.5K examples); authors argue (Spearman ~ -0.11, p>0.7) this does not drive results.
Restricted to two Gemma-3 model sizes and primarily one main domain (GSM8K), with limited additional Reasoning Gym evaluation.
Reasoning chains are produced by Gemma-3 27B and Gemini 2.5 Flash teachers; teacher choice may affect generated chain properties.
Threshold tau is the same absolute value across strategies, which the authors argue is needed for fair comparison but ignores ceiling differences.
Token perplexity baseline uses teacher-token likelihood of 1, not true teacher distribution, which may underestimate this baseline.
Results are correlational, not causal; the metric is not directly optimizable.
figures_tables: Figure 1: Overview illustrating different reasoning strategies, the LoRA-sweep procedure to compute intrinsic dimension, and the inverse correlation with generalization.
Figure 2: Pareto frontier of training accuracy vs trainable parameters for Gemma-3 4B across strategies, showing threshold crossings at tau=63%.
Figure 3: Analogous Pareto frontier for Gemma-3 1B at tau=24.3%.
Table 1: Main 4B results across 14 strategies, with ID/OOD/Overall accuracy and four baseline metrics; reports Spearman correlations (intrinsic dim 0.93).
Table 2: Same as Table 1 for Gemma-3 1B (intrinsic dim correlation 0.75).
Table 3: Threshold robustness analysis showing correlations across four threshold choices.
Table 4: Reasoning Gym results for algorithmic and cognitive task categories with metric correlations.
Table 5: Effect of 50% incorrect chains on intrinsic dim and accuracy.
Table 6: Easy vs hard configurations for Reasoning Gym tasks.
Tables 8-9: Detailed per-split accuracies for Gemma-3 4B and 1B.
equations_algorithms: LoRA parameterization: W = W0 + BA, with params(r, L_LoRA) = 2 * L_LoRA * d_model * r.
Intrinsic dimension definition: d_int = min{d : A(d) >= tau}, with tau a common threshold across reasoning strategies.
Threshold selection heuristic: tau = 90% of maximum epoch-1 full-capacity training accuracy across strategies (avoiding overfitting/memorization contamination).
Sequence KL divergence baseline: average sequence-level negative log-likelihood (1/N) sum_i -log pi_theta(y_i | x_i).
Greedy LoRA configuration selection algorithm: for each target parameter count, choose target-module group and rank minimizing |actual - target| parameter count.

parsed.field_and_subfield: Natural Language Processing / Analysis and Interpretability of Large Language Model Reasoning (Chain-of-Thought)
parsed.problem_addressed: Existing explanations of why some chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning strategies generalize better than others are either qualitative (e.g., 'structure', 'coherence') or rely on quantitative proxies (length, perplexity, reward models) that show conflicting evidence. There is no principled, quantitative measure that reliably predicts the downstream generalization benefit of training on a given reasoning strategy.
parsed.research_question: Can intrinsic dimensionality — the minimum number of trainable (LoRA) parameters needed to reach a fixed training-accuracy threshold — serve as a principled, quantitative predictor of which reasoning strategies will yield the best generalization when used as fine-tuning supervision?
parsed.one_paragraph_summary: The paper proposes intrinsic dimensionality, estimated via LoRA fine-tuning sweeps, as a measure of how 'learnable' a chain-of-thought reasoning strategy is for a fixed base model. Holding the model and task questions constant while varying only the reasoning-chain format (No CoT, Short CoT, distractor-injected CoT, Executed PoT, Plan-and-Solve, Critical CoT, High-Review-Ratio CoT, etc.), the authors fine-tune Gemma-3 1B and 4B with LoRA configurations spanning ~20–30 trainable-parameter budgets, and define a strategy's intrinsic dimension as the smallest budget that achieves a common accuracy threshold. Across 14 reasoning strategies on GSM8K and five OOD math stress tests, intrinsic dimension shows a strong inverse correlation with overall accuracy (Spearman 0.93 on 4B; 0.75 on 1B), substantially exceeding length (0.31/0.24), token perplexity (0.82/0.63), KL divergence (≈0), and LongPPL (0.60–0.62). Results are robust to threshold choice, hold on Reasoning Gym algorithmic and cognitive tasks (where No-CoT wins for cognitive, consistent with prior findings), and degrade gracefully when training data correctness is corrupted. The authors connect their findings to the Minimum Description Length principle: effective reasoning chains make the input→answer mapping more compressible.
parsed.problem_statement: Given multiple reasoning strategies that produce different rationales for the same set of problems, predict — without expensive test-time evaluation — which strategy will yield the best generalization when used as supervised fine-tuning data for a given LLM. The paper proposes intrinsic dimensionality (via LoRA sweeps) as such a predictor and validates it empirically.
main_contributions: Reformulating intrinsic dimensionality (originally used to characterize models given fixed data) to instead characterize reasoning strategies given a fixed model, by varying the rationale format used as training supervision.

An operational definition and estimation procedure: d_int = min{d : A(d) ≥ τ}, computed via a log-spaced LoRA sweep over rank and target-module subsets, with a common threshold τ derived from epoch-1 full-capacity training accuracy.

An empirical demonstration on Gemma-3 1B/4B and 14 reasoning strategies that intrinsic dimensionality is a substantially stronger predictor of in- and out-of-distribution generalization than length, token perplexity, sequence KL divergence, or LongPPL.
Robustness studies covering threshold selection, training data correctness (mixed correct/incorrect chains), and a second domain (Reasoning Gym algorithmic and cognitive tasks).

An MDL-grounded conceptual interpretation: effective reasoning chains, despite being longer outputs, render the underlying input→answer mapping more compressible, hence requiring fewer effective parameters to fit.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Intrinsic dimensionality strongly predicts generalization performance of reasoning strategies.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 1 (Gemma-3 4B): Spearman 0.93 (p<0.01) between intrinsic dim and overall accuracy across 14 strategies. Table 2 (Gemma-3 1B): Spearman 0.75 (p<0.05).

claim_evidence_map.claim: Intrinsic dimensionality outperforms alternative quantitative metrics (length, KL, token perplexity, LongPPL).
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Tables 1–2: length 0.31/0.24, token PPL 0.82/0.63, KL −0.17/−0.18. Table 7: LongPPL (K=64) 0.60, (K=256) 0.62, all lower than intrinsic dim 0.93 on 4B.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Findings are robust to the choice of accuracy threshold τ.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 3 shows correlations of 0.72–0.94 across thresholds at 70%/80%/90% of epoch-1 train accuracy and 90% of validation accuracy for both 1B and 4B.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Executed PoT is the most effective strategy and has the lowest intrinsic dimension.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 1: Executed PoT achieves 46.15 overall (best OOD 43.40) with intrinsic dim 1.49M; Figure 2 shows it crosses τ=63% earliest. Same pattern on 1B (Table 2).

claim_evidence_map.claim: Incorrect reasoning chains degrade both intrinsic dimension and generalization.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 5: mixing 50% incorrect Short CoT increases intrinsic dim from 3.92M to 2729.64M (~500×) and drops overall accuracy from 32.63 to 20.61; similar pattern for Gemma 27B CoT.

claim_evidence_map.claim: The framework generalizes beyond math to other reasoning domains.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Table 4: On Reasoning Gym algorithmic tasks, intrinsic dim correlates 1.00 with overall accuracy (Executed PoT best at 73.21). On cognitive tasks, No CoT achieves lowest intrinsic dim (0.40M) and best overall (49.82), with intrinsic dim correlation 1.00.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Larger models compress effective reasoning strategies more efficiently.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Comparing Tables 1 and 2: for Executed PoT, 4B reaches a higher absolute threshold (63%) with 1.49M parameters; 1B reaches a lower threshold (24.3%) with 1.03M; for ineffective strategies (Very Short CoT, distractors), 4B requires substantially more parameters than 1B.

claim_evidence_map.claim: Training-set size differences across strategies do not drive the results.
claim_evidence_map.evidence_in_paper: Appendix B.1: training set sizes range 6.2K–7.5K; Spearman between size and accuracy ≈ −0.11 (p>0.7); Executed PoT has the smallest training set yet best performance.

sections.name: Abstract
sections.summary: Introduces intrinsic dimensionality (minimum trainable params via LoRA to reach an accuracy threshold) as a measure of CoT effectiveness; claims a strong inverse correlation with generalization on GSM8K with Gemma-3 1B/4B.
key_claims: Effective reasoning strategies reduce intrinsic dimensionality.
Intrinsic dimensionality inversely correlates with ID and OOD generalization.
supporting_evidence: Spearman correlations reported in Tables 1-2 (0.93 for 4B, 0.75 for 1B).
issues: Abstract does not mention that intrinsic dimensionality is approximated via LoRA, which is only an upper bound.
Generalization claim is supported on a single primary dataset (GSM8K).
missing_information: No mention of scope (single domain primarily, two model sizes) or computational cost.

sections.name: Introduction
sections.summary: Frames the question of which CoT strategies are most effective, criticizes existing qualitative/quantitative measures, and motivates intrinsic dimensionality via MDL.
key_claims: Existing metrics (length, process reward models) provide conflicting or subjective evidence.
Intrinsic dimensionality is a principled MDL-based predictor of reasoning effectiveness.
supporting_evidence: Cites Muennighoff et al., Wu et al., Marjanovic et al. for conflicting length evidence.
issues: The link between MDL and intrinsic dimensionality is asserted but not formally derived; intrinsic dim as defined is a function of model + data fitting, not strictly a description-length measure.
Motivational claims about practical implications (data annotation, alignment) are not concretely demonstrated in the paper.
missing_information: No upfront acknowledgment of computational cost of computing intrinsic dim, which limits its practical utility.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Statement that intrinsic dimensionality is 'theoretically grounded' is rhetorical; no theorem is provided.

sections.name: Related Work
sections.summary: Surveys prior CoT evaluation and intrinsic dimension literature, positioning the paper as inverting prior intrinsic-dim setups by varying data rather than model.
key_claims: Prior CoT analyses are mostly non-quantifiable proxies.
Authors' use of intrinsic dim measures complexity of the learning objective, not the data manifold.
supporting_evidence: Cites Li et al. 2018, Aghajanyan et al. 2021, Hu et al. 2022, Blier & Ollivier 2018, Rissanen 1978.
issues: Could discuss more competing quantitative proxies (e.g., loss landscape sharpness, NTK-based measures).
Does not discuss potential confounds when comparing intrinsic dim across different output spaces (different output distributions, vocab usage).
missing_information: No discussion of measures from the literature on instance-level reasoning chain difficulty (e.g., information gain or step-level metrics) beyond brief mentions.

sections.name: Method
sections.summary: Defines intrinsic dimension as the minimum LoRA params needed to reach a common accuracy threshold tau; sweeps rank and module targets to span parameter counts; justifies using epoch-1 max training accuracy across strategies as the threshold.
key_claims: LoRA serves as a structured low-dimensional projection consistent with intrinsic dim framework.
Common absolute threshold across strategies is needed for fair comparison.
supporting_evidence: Algorithm and module-group definitions in Appendix A.1.
Robustness to threshold demonstrated in Table 3.
issues: The definition d_int = min{d : A(d) >= tau} uses *training* accuracy A(d), so it measures fitting capacity, not generalization directly; this is an important conceptual distinction not heavily emphasized.

Using a single shared absolute threshold tau penalizes strategies whose maximum attainable accuracy is below tau (they would have undefined or infinite intrinsic dim) — the paper does not clearly explain what happens for such strategies.
Greedy module-group selection means different intrinsic-dim points are achieved via qualitatively different adaptations (attention-only vs MLP-only), which may not be smoothly comparable.
LoRA initialization, training step budget, and hyperparameter sensitivity at small ranks may significantly affect threshold-crossing, especially for No CoT strategies.
missing_information: How are 'epoch 1' boundaries handled for different dataset sizes (6.2K-7.5K) — is one epoch the same number of steps?
Are reported intrinsic dimensions averaged over multiple seeds? Variance is not reported.
No description of selection rule when training accuracy curve crosses threshold non-monotonically (Figures 2-3 suggest monotonic Pareto frontier is used).
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim that LoRA aligns with intrinsic dimensionality framework is partly heuristic; the original intrinsic dim definition uses random projections, and LoRA's structured low-rank subspace is a different (and not strictly comparable) constraint.

sections.name: Experiments
sections.summary: 14 reasoning strategies are generated from teacher models (mostly Gemma-3 27B, plus Gemini 2.5 Flash) and used to fine-tune Gemma-3 1B/4B; baseline metrics include length, token PPL, KL divergence; evaluation on GSM8K + 5 OOD splits and Reasoning Gym.
key_claims: Strategies span diverse lengths and structures, enabling broad comparison.
Training set size differences do not drive results (Spearman ~ -0.11).
supporting_evidence: Appendix B.1 details data sizes and decontamination.
Appendix C describes Reasoning Gym configurations.
issues: Teacher choice is heterogeneous: most strategies come from Gemma-3 27B, but Gemini CoT comes from a stronger teacher; this confounds 'reasoning strategy' with 'teacher quality' (especially for the Gemini comparison).
Token perplexity baseline assumes teacher likelihood = 1, deliberately omitting teacher distribution — this biases the baseline against the metric and the authors acknowledge but do not address it.
Only one base model family (Gemma-3) is tested; results may not generalize to other architectures.
Only one math dataset is the primary subject, with Reasoning Gym serving as the only diversity test, evaluated on only 4 strategies.
No reported standard error or seed variance for any accuracy or intrinsic dim number.
missing_information: Specifics of how 'No CoT with extra tokens' filler is constructed and why it is a meaningful inference-time-compute control.
Whether evaluation uses greedy decoding, sampling, or self-consistency — could affect ID/OOD numbers.
Sequence KL formula given is just average NLL — equating this with KL requires an implicit assumption about the empirical distribution that should be stated more carefully.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Sequence KL = average NLL is technically the cross-entropy under uniform empirical distribution; calling it KL divergence is slightly misleading.

sections.name: Results
sections.summary: Intrinsic dimensionality has the strongest Spearman correlation with overall accuracy (0.93/0.75 for 4B/1B), substantially better than length, KL, and token PPL. Robust across thresholds, generalizes to Reasoning Gym, and worsens with incorrect chains.
key_claims: Intrinsic dim is the best predictor among tested metrics.
Larger models compress effective strategies more but require more capacity for ineffective ones.
Mixing incorrect chains greatly increases intrinsic dim and reduces accuracy.
issues: Spearman correlation over only 14 (Tables 1-2) or 4 (Table 4) strategies; with n=4 the p-values cannot be reliably significant and correlation = 1.0 is easy to attain by chance.

Many intrinsic dim values are tied across strategies (e.g., 2729.64, 532.81, 31.45, 7.34 appear repeatedly), suggesting the sweep grid is coarse; ranking ties may favor the metric's appearance of strong correlation but reduce its actual resolution.
Token PPL correlation (0.82) is close to intrinsic dim (0.93), and the paper does not test whether the difference is statistically significant.

Claim that 'larger models compress effective reasoning more efficiently' is supported by comparing Exec PoT 1.49M (4B) vs 1.03M (1B), but the threshold is also different (63% vs 24.3%), so this is not a clean comparison.

The incorrect-chain experiment increases intrinsic dim by 500x-125x but training accuracy may also drop (since the model cannot reach the same threshold) — it's unclear whether the threshold is held fixed or whether incorrect chains are even reachable to threshold; methodology should be clearer.

Cognitive Reasoning Gym results: No CoT wins with intrinsic dim 0.40M and best accuracy — but the input length is short and No CoT may simply benefit from the model's prior knowledge; correlation = -1.0 for length/PPL with only 4 points is not strong evidence.
missing_information: No confidence intervals or bootstrap p-values for Spearman correlations.
No analysis of which LoRA target modules dominate the optimal configurations across strategies (this could reveal whether intrinsic dim is really comparable across strategies).
No ablation on dataset size (e.g., subsampling to equal sizes across strategies).
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Comparing intrinsic dimensions across thresholds tau (4B vs 1B) cannot itself support the 'larger models compress more efficiently' claim without a controlled analysis.
Claim that intrinsic dim 'potentially captures coherence across the complete set of trajectories' is asserted but not analyzed.

sections.name: Limitations / Discussion / Conclusion
sections.summary: Acknowledges high computational cost of intrinsic dim estimation, LoRA giving an upper bound, and suggests future work on tractable proxies and other PEFT methods.
key_claims: LoRA-based intrinsic dim is an upper bound; alternative PEFT methods may give tighter bounds.
Intrinsic dim is correlational and computationally expensive.
supporting_evidence: Cites Lotfi et al. 2024 and Shaw et al. 2026 for tighter bounds.
issues: Does not address that the metric requires knowing the training data and running many fine-tunes, undermining practical utility (e.g., for selecting strategies before training).
No discussion of whether the metric could be confounded by teacher-style or stylistic features of training data unrelated to reasoning effectiveness.
No quantitative estimate of cost (GPU-hours) for the sweeps.
missing_information: No discussion of whether intrinsic dim transfers across base models (e.g., would the ranking of strategies be the same on a non-Gemma model?).
No discussion of potential application to RL/distillation setups beyond mentioning it as future work.

sections.name: Supplementary
sections.summary: Provides LoRA sweep algorithm, dataset details for GSM8K and Reasoning Gym, LongPPL comparison, examples of all 14 reasoning strategies, and detailed per-split accuracies.
key_claims: Training set size does not significantly correlate with accuracy (Spearman ~ -0.11).
LongPPL does not outperform plain token PPL in this setting.
supporting_evidence: Appendix B.1 dataset sizes; Appendix D LongPPL results; Tables 8-9 per-split breakdowns.
issues: The greedy configuration selection means many target parameter counts collapse to the same actual parameter count, leading to a small effective number of distinct intrinsic dim values.
Reasoning Gym setup uses only 4 strategies — too few for robust rank-correlation conclusions.
Example strategies are clear but do not include data filtering details (e.g., how many of teacher's chains were rejected for incorrectness).
missing_information: No information on validation sets used for checkpoint selection (size, sampling).
No description of seed averaging or variance for the LoRA training runs.
Per-strategy intrinsic dim curves (training accuracy vs params) are only shown for select strategies in Figures 2-3; full curves for all strategies are not provided.
ambiguous_or_unsupported: Claim of decontamination for Reasoning Gym OOD sets is described qualitatively but contamination test details are not provided.

claims.claim: Intrinsic dimensionality, measured via LoRA sweeps, strongly predicts the generalization performance of different chain-of-thought reasoning strategies on a fixed model.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 1 shows Spearman rank correlation 0.93 (p<0.01) between intrinsic dim and overall accuracy across 14 strategies on Gemma-3 4B; Table 2 shows 0.75 (p<0.05) on Gemma-3 1B.
claims.notes: Correlations are computed over only 14 points, which is small for statistical significance but p-values are reported. Stronger on 4B than 1B.

claims.claim: Intrinsic dimensionality outperforms baseline metrics (trajectory length, token perplexity, sequence KL divergence, LongPPL) at predicting reasoning effectiveness.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Tables 1, 2, 7: length 0.31/0.24, token PPL 0.82/0.63, KL −0.17/−0.18, LongPPL 0.60–0.62, vs intrinsic dim 0.93/0.75.
claims.notes: Comparison is fair within their evaluation framework; baselines are reasonable proxies.

claims.claim: The predictive power of intrinsic dimensionality is robust to the choice of accuracy threshold τ.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 3: correlations remain 0.72–0.94 across threshold variations (70%/80%/90% of epoch-1 train acc and 90% of val acc).
claims.notes: Robustness check is well-executed for threshold variation; doesn't address robustness to LoRA sweep choices.

claims.claim: Executed PoT (program-of-thought with execution) achieves the lowest intrinsic dimensionality and best OOD performance.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 1: Exec PoT 1.49M params, 46.15 overall, 43.40 OOD; Figure 2 shows earliest threshold crossing; consistent on 1B (Table 2).
claims.notes: Exec PoT's advantage may partly stem from the fact that the answer comes from external execution, sidestepping the model's arithmetic limitations—orthogonal to compression. The paper acknowledges this connection to Sprague et al. but doesn't fully disentangle it.

claims.claim: Incorrect reasoning chains substantially increase intrinsic dimensionality and reduce generalization.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 5: 50% incorrect Short CoT increases intrinsic dim ~500× (3.92M→2729.64M) and drops accuracy by 12 points; similar for Gemma 27B CoT (~125×, 12.8 point drop).
claims.notes: Only 2 strategies tested; limited but supportive.

claims.claim: The framework generalizes to non-math reasoning domains (Reasoning Gym algorithmic and cognitive tasks).
claims.evidence_in_paper: Table 4: Spearman correlation 1.00 for both algorithmic and cognitive categories (overall accuracy vs intrinsic dim).
claims.notes: Correlation of 1.00 is computed over only 4 strategies per category, which makes the rank correlation trivially achievable. This is far less convincing than the 14-strategy GSM8K results.

claims.claim: Larger models compress effective reasoning strategies more efficiently than smaller ones.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Comparing Tables 1/2: Exec PoT 4B uses 1.49M to hit 63% threshold; 1B uses 1.03M to hit 24.3%. Ineffective strategies show reverse pattern.
claims.notes: Comparison is confounded because the threshold τ differs between 1B and 4B; comparing parameter counts at different thresholds doesn't cleanly support the 'better compressor' claim. The qualitative argument has merit but the quantitative comparison is not apples-to-apples.

claims.claim: Differences in training-set sizes (6.2K–7.5K examples per strategy) do not drive the observed performance differences.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Appendix B.1: Spearman correlation between training size and accuracy ≈ −0.11 (p>0.7); Exec PoT smallest but best.
claims.notes: A reasonable control. The negative correlation actually slightly favors smaller datasets, addressing a natural confound.

claims.claim: Effective reasoning chains 'compress' the input→answer mapping in an MDL sense, requiring fewer parameters to learn.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section 6 connects findings to MDL principle (Rissanen, 1978; Grünwald, 2007); intrinsic dimension framed as proxy for description length.
claims.notes: This is an interpretive narrative rather than a formal theoretical result. The link between LoRA parameter count and MDL description length is not formalized, and the paper itself notes LoRA only provides an upper bound that 'may be loose'.

claims.claim: Intrinsic dimensionality is a novel metric for characterizing reasoning effectiveness.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Related work (Section 5) contrasts the approach with prior intrinsic dim work (Li et al., 2018; Aghajanyan et al., 2021), which varied models on fixed data; this work fixes the model and varies the rationale format.
claims.notes: Novelty primarily lies in the application/reformulation rather than the underlying technique.

claims.claim: Intrinsic dimensionality can be computed from training curves alone without test-set evaluation, providing practical utility.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section 2.3 and 4.3: τ derived from epoch-1 maximum training accuracy across strategies; Table 3 shows this works robustly.
claims.notes: While technically true, computing intrinsic dim requires running 20–30 LoRA fine-tuning sweeps per strategy, which is expensive. The paper acknowledges this in Section 6 as a limitation.

claims.claim: Token perplexity and intrinsic dimensionality are interrelated because compressible chains are also high-likelihood under the base model.
claims.evidence_in_paper: Section 4.1 cites Yue et al. (2026); token PPL achieves 0.82 vs intrinsic dim 0.93.
claims.notes: This is an informal argument rather than a derived relationship; serves more as intuition than evidence.

unsupported_claims: The Reasoning Gym correlations of 1.00 are presented as supporting cross-domain generalization but are trivial given only 4 data points per category.
Claim that larger models 'compress effective reasoning more efficiently' compares parameter counts across different accuracy thresholds, making the comparison ill-defined.
overstated_claims: Intrinsic dimensionality 'captures coherence across the complete set of trajectories' (Section 6) — this is speculation, not demonstrated.
Framing of intrinsic dimension as MDL-grounded — the connection is intuitive but not formally established; LoRA only provides an upper bound that may be loose.

'Strong inverse correlation' on 1B (0.75) is presented at equal weight to 4B (0.93), but on 1B several strategies cluster at near-floor performance (1–10% overall), which inflates rank correlation given the order is dominated by which strategies are above floor.

Claim that the metric provides 'a principled, quantitative predictor' implies practical applicability, but computing it requires 20–30 full LoRA fine-tunes per strategy, undermining its utility for selection.
claims_requiring_external_verification: Whether the chosen learning rates and training duration (8K steps for 1B, 6K for 4B) actually let smaller LoRA configurations converge sufficiently — undertraining at low ranks could artificially inflate intrinsic dimension estimates for some strategies.
Whether the relative ordering of strategies by intrinsic dimension is stable under alternative PEFT methods (the paper acknowledges this as future work).
Whether the correlation pattern holds on non-Gemma model families, given the conflict-of-interest disclosure.
Whether intrinsic dimensionality correlations remain strong on larger, more diverse strategy sets and non-arithmetic tasks beyond Reasoning Gym.

novelty_axes.claimed_contribution: Using LoRA-rank sweeps to measure the intrinsic dimensionality of a learning task while fixing the model and varying the training data (the reasoning strategy / rationale format), rather than the prior setting that varies the model on fixed data.
novelty_axes.why_it_matters: The paper's main technical contribution is the *inversion* of the intrinsic-dimensionality probe: if this exact setup (LoRA sweep over fixed model, varying rationales) already exists, the methodological novelty largely collapses to an application.

similar_prior_work.title: Intrinsic Dimensionality Explains the Effectiveness of Language Model Fine-Tuning
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Introduces the technique of measuring intrinsic dimensionality of a task via low-dimensional reparameterization (and discusses LoRA-style projections); shows large pretrained models have low intrinsic dim for downstream tasks.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Varies the model (pretraining scale / data) at fixed task data; this paper inverts the design — fixes the model and varies the *task formulation* by changing rationale style, and uses that to rank reasoning strategies.

similar_prior_work.title: Measuring the Intrinsic Dimension of Objective Landscapes
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Foundational paper defining intrinsic dimensionality of a learning objective via random low-dimensional projections.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: Defines the concept and protocol the paper inherits; the present work substitutes LoRA for random projections and re-purposes the probe for reasoning-strategy comparison.

similar_prior_work.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
similar_prior_work.similarity_summary: Provides the parameter-efficient adapter used as the dimensionality-reduction operator P(·) in this paper's intrinsic-dim sweep.
similar_prior_work.key_difference: LoRA is a fine-tuning method, not a measurement tool; the paper uses LoRA rank as a knob to sweep the trainable-parameter budget and read off d_int.
